content_markdown:
A beaker containing $1\text{ kg}$ of water at room temperature is heated on a $400\text{ W}$ hot plate. The specific heat capacity of water is $4200\text{ J kg}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$.

The temperature of the water increases until it reaches a constant value. It is then removed from the hot plate.

What will be the initial rate of change of temperature?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

vibe_explanation: When the water reaches a constant temperature, the rate of energy gain from the hot plate equals the rate of energy loss to the surroundings. At the moment it is removed, the heating power ($400\text{ W}$) stops, but the cooling rate remains the same as it was at the steady state. Using $P = mc\frac{\Delta T}{\Delta t}$, we have $400 = 1 \times 4200 \times \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta t}$. Solving for the rate gives $\frac{400}{4200} \approx 0.095\text{ K s}^{-1}$, which rounds to $0.1\text{ K s}^{-1}$.
